coredata.dc:title: Combustion synthesis of TiO2/C composites for enhanced photocatalytic H2O2 production and solar steam evaporation efficiency
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

Cao, Yali
Feng, Yuanyuan
Zhu, Wenmiao
Li, Qinying
Gao, Meichao
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a simple combustion method was proposed for the controllable synthesis of TiO2/C composites, which can achieve both the efficient solar water evaporation at the interface and photocatalytic H2O2 production. Structural and morphological characteristic indicated the co-existence of carbon quantum dots and amorphous carbon, which were well distributed on the surface of the TiO2. TiO2/C composites displayed the enhanced visible light absorption and the rapid separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. TiO2/C-10 exhibited an excellent photocatalytic H2O2 production activity of 3068.46 μmol/g/h under the simulated solar irradiation. Besides, its water evaporation rate reached up to 5.81 kg·m−2·h−1 under the simulated solar irradiation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: TiO2/C
Composites
Solar steam evaporation
Photocatalytic H2O2 production

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illumination of the preparation of TiO2/C composites; (b) XRD pattern of the prepared samples (A: anatase; R: rutile); (c) TEM image and (d-f) HRTEM images of TiO2/C-10.

(a) The photocatalytic production of H2O2 from the prepared samples; (b) The rate of H2O2 production from the prepared samples.; (c)The cycle experiment of H2O2 production over TiO2/C-10. (d) Photocatalytic H2O2 production over TiO2/C-10 in the presence of different reactive species scavengers.

(a) Schematic of the solar water evaporation system. (b) Temperature change of TiO2 and TiO2-10 under solar irradiation. (c) Temperature change of pure water, TiO2 and TiO2/C-10 during the experiment. (d) Mass change of evaporated water for samples prepared; (e) The rate of evaporation of water from the prepared samples and (f) The rate of evaporation of water of TiO2/C-10 for 4 cycles, under solar intensity of 660 mW·cm−2.

head.ce:title: Combustion synthesis of TiO2/C composites for enhanced photocatalytic H2O2 production and solar steam evaporation efficiency

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Catalytic Conversion and Clean Energy in Universities of Shandong Province, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qufu Normal University, Qufu 273165, Shandong, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Catalytic Conversion and Clean Energy in Universities of Shandong Province
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Catalytic Conversion and Clean Energy in Universities of Shandong Province, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qufu Normal University, Qufu 273165, Shandong, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a simple combustion method was proposed for the controllable synthesis of TiO2/C composites, which can achieve both the efficient solar water evaporation at the interface and photocatalytic H2O2 production. Structural and morphological characteristic indicated the co-existence of carbon quantum dots and amorphous carbon, which were well distributed on the surface of the TiO2. TiO2/C composites displayed the enhanced visible light absorption and the rapid separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. TiO2/C-10 exhibited an excellent photocatalytic H2O2 production activity of 3068.46 μmol/g/h under the simulated solar irradiation. Besides, its water evaporation rate reached up to 5.81 kg·m−2·h−1 under the simulated solar irradiation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Solar water evaporation serves as a promising solution to combat freshwater scarcity through renewable energy. But it still faces significant challenges, e.g., while effective in desalinating seawater and purifying contaminated water, residual heavy metals and organic pollutants can compromise water quality, especially if the latter are volatile and evaporate alongside water. Additionally, many experimental setups utilize hydrophilic substrates or self-standing photothermal materials, risking structural failure that may lead to these materials sinking, thus reducing efficiency and possibly polluting the water [1]. A potential strategy is the degradation of organic contaminants in situ during the interfacial evaporation process. H2O2 is a potent oxidizing agent that may enhance the degradation of organic contaminants [2]. Therefore, one method to mitigate above these issues is the incorporation of submerged photothermal materials with photocatalytic in-situ H2O2 generation. While the key challenges in single device development involve enhancing materials with dual functionalities, which can be used as the solar-thermal conversion material and used as the catalyst for the photocatalytic H2O2 production.

Photocatalysis efficiently degrades toxic contaminants through the generation of free radicals, offering a green, low-cost, and effective technology. Semiconductor-based photocatalysis utilizes light energy to produce reactive species, such as •O2 −and •OH, which exhibit high oxidation and reduction capacity for the complete mineralization of pollutants [3]. The mechanism involves the generation of electron-hole pairs that facilitate redox reactions at the semiconductor surface [4]. TiO2 as a popular photocatalyst has the advantages of stability, cost-effectiveness, high catalytic activity, corrosion resistance, and environmental friendliness. In addition, TiO2-based materials have excellent activity for photocatalytic H2O2 production such as Au/TiO2-Bi2O3 [5], Pt and Co polyoxometalate modified TiO2 [6], TiO2/In2S3 [7] and TiO2/gC3N4 [8]. For the research of solar water evaporation, carbon materials commonly used include carbon nanotubes, graphene, and carbon nanoparticles. These carbon materials have excellent photothermal conversion properties and visible light absorption capability, making them suitable for enhancing the efficiency of solar thermal evaporation [9]. Simultaneously, carbon-based materials are extensively utilized in photocatalysis. Thus, the carbon and TiO2 composite can potentially result in efficient dual-functional materials, harnessing the synergistic effects of both components for enhanced performance including photocatalysis and solar-thermal evaporation.

In this study, we synthesized carbon loaded TiO2 composites with dual functionalities using a simple and rapid combustion method. The carbon quantum dots and amorphous carbon were well distributed on the surface of TiO2, demonstrating the successful synthesis of TiO2/C composites. As expected, it can not only achieve a water evaporation rate of 5.81 kg/m2/h but also produce 3068.46 μmol/g/h of H2O2 under simulated solar irradiation.

The synthetic route of TiO2/C composites was shown in Fig. 1 a. 0.2 g of sucrose was dissolved in 30 mL of deionized water. Then 2 g of TiO2 was placed in the sucrose solution and sonicated for 10 min; Subsequently, the solution was transfered to an electric jacket and heated until completely dry. After cooling, the sample was collected, ground and labeled with the sample name TiO2/C-10. A series of different samples can be prepared by controlling the proportions of TiO2 and sucrose in the reaction. Among them, the samples added with 2 g, 1 g, 0.6 g, 0.333 g, 0.2 g, 0.1667 g, 0.1333 g, 0.1 g of sucrose were named TiO2/C-1, TiO2/C-2, TiO2/C-3, TiO2/C-6, TiO2/C-10, TiO2/C-12, TiO2/C-15 and TiO2/C-20 respectively.

XRD patterns of the prepared samples are illustrated in Fig. 1b. The strong diffraction peaks of TiO2 (P25) indicated that the prepared samples were highly crystalline, composed of a mixture of tetragonal anatase (JCPDS No.84–1285) and tetragonal rutile (JCPDS No.89–4202). As demonstrated by the XRD pattern of TiO2 (P25), where the diffraction peaks at 25.3°, 37.8°, 47.9°, 53.9° and 54.9° corresponded well to (101), (004), (200), (105) and (211) planes of anatase and the diffraction peaks at 27.4°, 36.1°, 41.2°, 54.3° and 56.6° were consistent with (110), (101), (111), (211) and (220) planes of rutile. Compared with TiO2 (P25), the peaks of TiO2/C-2 and TiO2/C-10 become slightly weaker due to the adhesion of carbon on the surface. TEM and HRTEM were characterized to investigate the surface and interface structure of TiO2/C-10. Fig. 1c shows that many carbon quantum dots are well distributed on the surface of TiO2. The average diameter was 3 nm and the lattice fringes display that the carbon quantum dots have an inner-plane spacing of 0.33 nm corresponding to the (002) graphitic planes (Fig. 1d) [10]. Furthermore, it has been found that the amorphous carbon layer coated on the surface of TiO2 (Fig. 1e-f). It confirms the co-existence of nanocrystals and amorphous carbon.

As displayed in Fig. 2 a, the photocatalytic H2O2 production of as-prepared photocatalysts was tested in an oxygen saturated atmosphere under simulated solar irradiation. Obviously, TiO2/C-10 exhibited a most outstanding H2O2 productivity of 3068.46 μmol/g/h. For composite TiO2/C-10, comparative experiments of H2O2 generation under different conditions were performed as shown in Fig. 2b. The H2O2 evolution was greatly suppressed without light, catalyst, sacrificial agent or oxygen, indicating that it is a photocatalytic process and photocatalytic H2O2 production is highly dependent on atmospheric conditions during the reaction and O2 is the key parameter for improved H2O2 production. Subsequently, photogenerated electrons react with O2 to form H2O2 via the indirect 2e− oxygen reduction reaction pathway (O2 → •O2 − → H2O2). In addition, cycling experiments showed that the catalyst had good stability and recyclability (Fig. 2c). To reveal the main active species involved in the reaction during photocatalysis, we conducted reactive active species capture experiments using p-benzoquinone (p-BQ), isopropanol (IPA) and ammonium oxalate (AO) as scavengers of •O2 – (superoxide radical), •OH (hydroxyl radical) and h+ (hole), respectively. As shown in the Fig. 2d, the addition of PBQ and AO significantly reduced the photocatalytic efficiency, confirming that •O2 – and h+ are essential in the photocatalytic process. Consequently, photogenerated electrons can react with O2 to form H2O2 via the indirect oxygen reduction reaction pathway (O2→•O2−→H2O2) and photogenerated holes can react with H2O to form H2O2 via the direct water oxidation reaction pathway (H2O → H2O2) [11].

As shown in Fig. 3 a, a photothermal conversion device designed with TiO2/C-10 as the light absorption layer combined with polyethylene foam and mixed cellulose ester (MCE) membrane as the insulation and water transport layers, respectively. Fig. 3b and Fig. 3c displayed the surface temperature increases sharply from 20 to 62 °C for TiO2/C-10 at 6.6 sun intensity. This rapid rise in temperature can be explained to the enhanced light absorption (Fig. S5), which can be absorb and converted into thermal energy. By the blank control, the amount of water vapor produced after one hour was 1.24 kg·m−2. And the amount of water vapor produced by single MCE and pure TiO2 was 2.99 kg·m−2 and 2.18 kg·m−2, respectively, under the solar irradiation with 6.6 sun intensity (660 mW·cm−2) (Fig. 3d-e). TiO2/C-10 showed the best solar water evaporation performance with 5.81 kg·m−2 of evaporated water in one hour when the dosing amount of TiO2/C-10 was 25 mg. In addition, to investigate the stability of the TiO2/C-10 performance, four cycles experiments were done under the same conditions. The results showed that TiO2/C-10 has excellent cycle stability and can maintain a water evaporation rate of about 5.81 kg·m−2·h−1 in all four experiments (Fig. 3f).

In this work, we engineered an TiO2/C composites evaporator capable of simultaneous solar steam evaporation and photocatalytic H2O2 production. Structural and morphological characteristic indicated the co-existence of carbon quantum dots and amorphous carbon, which were well distributed on the surface of the TiO2. The photocatalytic experimental results showed that TiO2/C-10 had the best H2O2 production performance of 3068.46 μmol/g/h with good stability and recyclability. Moreover, •O2 – and h+ are essential intermediates in the photocatalytic process. Furthermore, the solar evaporation efficiency of TiO2/C-10 can reach up to 5.81 kg·m−2·h−1. The design of this multifunctional material could offer a straightforward and sustainable approach for both the applications of solar energy and the remediation of water resources.

Yunyun Gong: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Resources, Methodology, Data curation. Yali Cao: Software, Investigation, Conceptualization. Yuanyuan Feng: Validation, Project administration, Methodology. Wenmiao Zhu: Visualization, Software, Methodology, Investigation. Qinying Li: Visualization, Validation, Project administration, Data curation. Meichao Gao: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Project administration, Investigation, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22072070) and the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (ZR2021QE032), and the Scientific Research Foundation of Qufu Normal University (615101).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matlet.2024.137617.
